quotes.verbatim_quote:
In order to work out these vamps, I will have to introduce a few new chords. We will call them chords No. 27,

28, 29 and 30.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Extends the numbered chord catalog beyond 26, adding the four open-string-root voicings used for horn-backing fill-ins.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This can’t be done so easily in the key of C major, so we move up to the key of G major.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Explains why the fill-in vamps abandon the prior C-major workspace - the new chord shapes depend on guitar-friendly open-string roots only available in G.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
ke each one separately and transpose it to as many keys

ou have learned these vamps in Key of G major ta

be done separately, because you can’t play them all in every key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Acknowledges a critical limit on the transposition mandate - open-string vamp shapes are key-specific and must be selected per key rather than uniformly moved.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Now we will work with bridges.
In this Lesson I have written out three of the most used bridges in Jazz. They are written as always, standard chords at the top, and new chords at the bottom of each example.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Names bridges as a distinct study category and confirms the standard/new two-line presentation format extends to them.

quotes.verbatim_quote: If you should come across a few chord progressions that are very hard to play, work on them continually until you can play them comfortably.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Sets the comfort-with-difficulty study standard for the bridge material.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This Lesson is the key to all of the other lessons. Here I have written out the best possible way to connect one chord to another using the dominant as leading chord for each example.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Elevates Lesson 16 to capstone status and names the dominant-as-connector principle as the unifying theory behind every progression in the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote: First we have dominant to dominant connections, second, we have dominant to minor connections and third, dominant to major connections. With these few chord connections you can build progressions around any song.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the three-category taxonomy (V-to-V, V-to-minor, V-to-major) that organises Baker's universal-progression toolkit.

quotes.verbatim_quote: All you have to do is keep working with them until you find the one that fits best. As you work with these chords you will begin to develop an ear for them. Then as soon as you hear a song you will know just what chord connections to use.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the pedagogical end-goal: drilling chord-connection patterns trains the ear to select reharmonisations on the fly from any heard tune.
